cards.definition: The smallest unit of text a model processes: a word, sub-word, character, or piece of punctuation, each with an integer ID.
Breaking a body of text into tokens so the model can process and count them.
A vector that encodes a token's semantic and linguistic meaning; tokens used in similar contexts get similar vector directions.
The transformer mechanism that weights how much each surrounding token influences the current token when building its embedding.
The architecture behind modern LLMs: an encoder builds embeddings via attention; a decoder predicts the next token.
Encoder creates context-aware embeddings using attention; decoder uses them to predict the next most probable token, one at a time.
Information added alongside token vectors telling the transformer where each token sits in the sequence, because order changes meaning.
The output an LLM generates in response to a prompt, produced one predicted token at a time.
Retrieving relevant documents and adding them to the prompt so the model's answer is grounded in your data instead of generic.
A measure (0-1) of how aligned two vectors are; near 1 means very similar meaning, near 0 means unrelated.
Term Frequency x Inverse Document Frequency: scores a word high when it is frequent in one document but rare across the corpus, so common words score near zero.
Stemming chops word endings (running -> run); lemmatisation reduces a word to its dictionary form using linguistic rules (better -> good).
Representing text as a vector of word frequencies, ignoring grammar and order; input for classifiers like Naive Bayes.
An unsupervised, graph-based algorithm (PageRank for text) that ranks sentences to produce an extractive summary.
Extractive selects existing sentences; abstractive generates new wording that captures the themes.
Identifying the main concepts or talking points in unstructured text.
Finding named entities in text: people, places, organisations, dates, and quantities.
Classifying text by emotional tone as positive, negative, or neutral; a special case of text classification.
Finding (and optionally redacting) personally identifiable information such as names, phone numbers, and addresses in text.
Identifying the language of a document, returning an ISO code and a confidence score; often the first step in a text pipeline.
Detecting and reading text within images or scanned documents; the first stage of information extraction.
Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients: features (~13 per frame) extracted from audio that mimic how the human ear perceives sound.
A distinct unit of sound in speech (~44 in English); acoustic models predict phonemes from audio.
In speech-to-text, the acoustic model converts audio to phonemes; the language model maps phonemes to the most likely words.
A decoding method that keeps a shortlist of the best partial transcriptions, balancing acoustic fidelity against grammar; trades accuracy for latency.
The rhythm, stress, and intonation of speech; flat prosody is what makes synthesised voices sound robotic.
Converting written letters to their spoken sounds, handling English's inconsistent spelling (though, through, cough).
A model (e.g., WaveNet, HiFi-GAN) that converts mel-spectrograms into raw audio waveforms in text-to-speech.
A text-to-speech voice that produces more natural, human-like intonation than older synthesis methods.
Azure's managed service combining speech-to-text, AI reasoning, and text-to-speech into one conversational API.
Image generation that starts from random noise and iteratively removes it, guided by the prompt, until an image forms.
Convolutional Neural Network: uses learned filters to extract image features, then a neural network classifies them.
A transformer applied to image patches instead of text tokens, using attention to embed visual feature relationships.
A model that understands multiple data types at once (e.g., text + image), enabling image description and visual Q&A.

cards.term: Image classification vs object detection vs segmentation
cards.definition: Classification gives one whole-image label; object detection locates objects with bounding boxes; segmentation labels every pixel.
A generation parameter controlling randomness: higher is more creative/varied, lower is more focused and deterministic.
A cap on the length of a model's response; setting it too low truncates answers.
A deployment's throughput/capacity limit; exceeding it causes throttling, eased by lowering max_tokens or concurrency.
The system prompt is the developer's instruction setting behaviour and constraints; the user prompt is the end user's specific question.
A model packaged with instructions and tools (often knowledge) that can break a goal into steps and take actions.
Tools are callable actions (code, APIs, MCP servers); knowledge is documents/data retrieved to ground responses via RAG.
An open standard letting agents discover and call external tools and data sources without custom integration code.
Raw models are called via the OpenAI Responses API; agents are called via the Foundry Project API with an agent reference.
A large pretrained model (GPT, Claude, Mistral) offering general capabilities out of the box, usable directly or fine-tuned.
Large Language Models are powerful and general but costlier; Small Language Models are cheaper and suit focused or on-device use.
Azure's schema-driven service that extracts structured data (JSON) from documents, audio, and video, beyond basic OCR.
A schema defines what fields to extract and how to structure them; an analyzer applies that schema consistently to produce results.
Fairness, reliability & safety, privacy & security, inclusiveness, transparency, and accountability.
